coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot with aggregation induced emission and pH triggered disintegration
coredata.dc:creator: Nayak, Suman
Das, Prolay
Singh, Manoj K.
coredata.dc:description: Reportedly, Aggregation Induced Emission (AIE) from Carbon Dots (CDs) is mainly induced by hydrophobic interactions. Herein, we report AIE from pristine CDs that are predominantly hydrophilic. A multistage purification protocol was employed to separate the pristine CD sample from byproducts and CD mixture. At neutral pH, the aggregated CDs exhibit a large Stokes shift with absorption maxima at 405 nm and emission in the yellow region (λmax- 535 nm) which decays mono-exponentially with a time constant of 4.03 ns. The AIE from CD quenches on moving towards the acidic region. Significant changes in the UV–visible absorption and emission spectrum are seen on increasing the pH towards alkaline medium. While the pH-induced changes in the acidic medium are reversible in nature, the emission turns blue (λmax ~ 430 nm) in alkaline medium and the process is irreversible. Microscopic and Light Scattering studies suggest pH triggered disintegration of aggregates in alkaline medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Aggregation induced emission (AIE)
pH sensing
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Purification, optical absorption and luminescence of CD
Structure, morphology and functional group characterization

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are thankful to IIT Patna and BARC, Mumbai for infrastructure and experimental facilities. SN is thankful to IIT Patna for fellowship. Special thanks to Dr. P. A. Hassan, BARC for his help in DLS measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. Agarose gel electrophoresis image showing difference in electrophoretic mobility of YCD and BCD. B. Reverse Phase HPLC profile of gel extracted pure YCD. C. UV–Vis Absorption (black), PL Excitation (blue) and Emission spectra (red) of YCD. D. Photoluminescence decay curve of pure YCD at neutral pH. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Characterization of YCD A. TEM image (inset, SAED pattern). B. HRTEM image. C. AFM image with height profile and D. XRD pattern.
A. FTIR spectrum of YCD at neutral pH. B. XPS survey spectrum showing the presence of C, O, and N in YCD and deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C1s (C), O1s (D), and N1s (E).

A. UV–Vis absorption spectra of YCD at different pH conditions (7.2 → 11.5). B. PL emission spectra of YCD during pH variation (λex- 405 nm). C. Estimation of pKa from variation in OD at 325 nm (Inset shows variation at405nm). D. Variation in PL intensity at 535 nmunder different pH conditions. E. Digital images of CD solutions taken during pH variation on illumination by a UV lamp.

A. PL emission of YCD at different pH conditions (~7.4 → 1.4) (inset: UV–Vis absorption spectra). B. Variation of PL intensity at 535 nm during acid titration. C. Digital images CD solutions at different pH on illumination by a UV- lamp.
A. FTIR Spectra of CD at different pH conditions. B. Schematic presentation of the energy states of CD in the free and aggregated form.
Schematic showing the synthesis, aggregation induces emission and pH triggered disintegration of CD.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot with aggregation induced emission and pH triggered disintegration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Atomic and Molecular Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Atomic and Molecular Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Atomic and Molecular Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reportedly, Aggregation Induced Emission (AIE) from Carbon Dots (CDs) is mainly induced by hydrophobic interactions. Herein, we report AIE from pristine CDs that are predominantly hydrophilic. A multistage purification protocol was employed to separate the pristine CD sample from byproducts and CD mixture. At neutral pH, the aggregated CDs exhibit a large Stokes shift with absorption maxima at 405 nm and emission in the yellow region (λmax- 535 nm) which decays mono-exponentially with a time constant of 4.03 ns. The AIE from CD quenches on moving towards the acidic region. Significant changes in the UV–visible absorption and emission spectrum are seen on increasing the pH towards alkaline medium. While the pH-induced changes in the acidic medium are reversible in nature, the emission turns blue (λmax ~ 430 nm) in alkaline medium and the process is irreversible. Microscopic and Light Scattering studies suggest pH triggered disintegration of aggregates in alkaline medium.

Aggregation induced yellow emission from hydrophilic disintegration for blue emissive transformation carbon dot (CD) and its pH mediated disintegration for blue emissive transformation.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: The most studied member of the carbon nanofamily, Carbon Dots (CDs) has emerged as an excellent PL nanomaterial prompting its potential applications in optoelectronics, bio-imaging, sensing, and others [1–6]. The exact mechanism of PL behavior of CD remains elusive, although significant progress has been made in deciphering the same. There is mounting evidence that emission arises from both an intrinsic bandgap resulting from confined sp2 conjugation in the core of CDs and extrinsic fluorescence caused by surface states. Therefore, the tunable fluorescence emission of CDs can be achieved by either controlling the domain size of sp2 conjugation or modifying the chemical groups formed on the surface of CDs. The surface-related defect states have imperfect sp2 domains due to the presence of functional groups connected with the carbon backbone. The synergistic sp2 and sp3 hybridization between the carbon backbones and the pendant functional groups act as PL origins. This signifies the vital role of the surface functional groups in modulating the PL of CDs [7]. Band-gap and wave-function engineering by heteroatom doping and surface functionalization has been found to alter the emissive state of CDs although success beyond doubt is yet to be achieved [8,9]. In fact, Photoluminescence from a particular CD is a combination of several factors making it so intriguing yet incompletely understood that depends highly on the nature of precursor used, atomic ratio, temperature, and other synthesis methodology [10,11].

An important factor that affects the overall characteristics of as-prepared CD which is often ignored is the presence of small fluorescent impurities or reaction byproducts formed during the synthesis of CD, mostly through bottom-up synthesis [12–14]. In a landmark report, Essner et al. [15] reviewed the purification techniques of more than 550 publications on fluorescent CDs and brought to attention that the literature on CDs is continuously being filled by “uncritical, unsubstantiated, and detrimental claims and misconceptions” which mostly arose from “inadequate and unsubstantiated” sample purification methods. This necessitates complete removal of the impurities/byproducts that prompts the adoption of various purification methods like dialysis, centrifugation, solvent extraction, gel electrophoresis, and chromatographic techniques [16,17]. We hypothesize that preparative scale agarose gel electrophoresis that is specifically used to resolve DNA, could be an excellent choice during multistep purification of CD. Notably, the water-soluble luminescent CD was first detected and separated as a fragment during purification of single-walled carbon nanotube by gel electrophoresis [18]. Elimination of impurities from the synthesized CD has enabled us to divulge an interesting mechanistic perspective of PL properties of our CD through the addition or eliminations of protons.

Herein, we demonstrate visually detectable pH-dependent modulation of PL from CD associated with Aggregation Induced Emission (AIE) that was first conceptualized by Luo et al. in 2001 [19]. The first observation of AIE enhancement from the surfactant-assisted synthesis of CD was reported by Gao et al [20]. followed by similar observations by many other researchers [21]. Some groups also reported hydrophobic interactions directed and/or concentration modulated AIE from CD where separate emission band in the longer wavelength was observed [22–28]. However, AIE from hydrophilic CD has not been reported earlier with conviction. Through this study, we establish that AIE from hydrophilic pristine CDs is also possible that display pH guided PL modulation via a synergy of physical and chemical processes. Although, pH-dependent variation of the PL properties of CD is reported in some cases where protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups on the CDs play an important role [29–33], such pH changes leading to AIE mediated tuning of PL of CD has not been observed so far. Additionally, unlike most literature reports, CD synthesized by us was purified in 4-stages which exhibit excitation independent PL with a single exponential decay giving strong yellow AIE at neutral pH. Interestingly, the pH-triggered disintegration of the aggregated CD in alkaline medium results in typical blue emission exhibited by most of the CDs that can be visually ascertained after irradiating with a UV light source. To the best of our knowledge, dual-mode pH-sensitive emission from hydrophilic CD coupled with AIE is being reported for the first time that may aid potential futuristic optoelectronic applications including pH induced color conversion through chemical transformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification, optical absorption and luminescence of CD
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal synthesis of CD from Ammonium citrate dibasic and Urea in the molar ratio of 1:3 yielded a mixture of CDs. The major product, a yellow emitting CD was extracted after successive purifications by microfiltration, followed by dialysis and finally separation through a silica column. The column-purified CD was characterized by optical spectroscopic techniques. The UV–Vis spectrum shows an intense peak at 405 nm with a small hump at 325 nm in addition to the absorption peaks below 300 nm. The PL was found to be excitation dependent as depicted in Fig.S1 A and B of the supporting information (SI). Evidently, there were two distinct states with absorption maxima near 325 and 405 nm which emit around 430 nm and 535 nm respectively. By careful analysis of absorption, emission and PL excitation results, it was apparent that the emission is not associated with multiple emitting states of the same CD rather there are two distinct species present in the medium. Gel electrophoresis technique in a preparative scale was employed to separate them out. As suspected, in addition to the yellow emitting CD (YCD) some blue emitting CD (BCD) were also present (Fig. 1A). The RP-HPLC of the gel extracted YCD (Fig. 1B) showed a sharp peak at ~ 15 min with peak purity index 0.99 indicating good homogeneity of the sample. Optical absorption, emission, and fluorescence excitation spectrum of YCD in aqueous medium at neutral pH are presented in Fig. 1C. Photoluminescence from the YCD was excitation independent having emission maxima at 535 nm. The PL decays mono-exponentially with a time constant of 4.03 ns (Fig. 1D) that confirmed the purity of the gel extracted CD. Furthermore, in the solid state YCD exhibit similar PL excitation and emission spectra as obtained in the aqueous medium at neutral pH. The solid sample placed in a cetrifuse tube and petri dish gives brilliant yellow fluorescence of the YCD upon UV (365 nm) illumination (Fig. S2, SI). The BCD appears less polar and its solubility is relatively poor in an aqueous medium. For comparison, optical spectroscopic studies of the blue-emitting CD are also performed. The BCD displayed a long-wavelength absorption peak at 325 nm and emission maxima ~ 430 nm (Fig. S3A and B, SI). The time-resolved PL of BCD fits to a bi-exponential decay function giving time constants of 0.58 ns and 7.9 ns respectively (Fig. S3C, SI).

Though PL of CD is still a less understood property, many studies confirm beyond doubt that the absorption band in the 200–300 nm region as detected in the present case also for both BCD and YCD, can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the sp2-hybridiesd core state. Whereas, the absorption band at 325 nm in BCD could be assigned to n-π* transition associated with the surface functional groups of the CD. However, the YCD does not have any transition in this region and the absorption maxima is at 405 nm that emits in the yellow region (λmax - 535 nm). The fluorescence quantum yield of YCD is estimated to be ~20% in aqueous medium, which increases up to 70% in polar aprotic solvents. We also investigated the effect of concentration on the absorption and emission characteristics of YCD in the 0.03 mg/ml to 5.15 mg/ml range. The OD at absorption maxima (405 nm) and PL emission intensity (λmax - 535 nm) increased linearly with respect to YCD concentration without any change in the shape or position of the spectra. (Fig. S4, SI). However, at extremely higher concentration (5.15 mg/ml) the increase in PL intensity reduced due to inner filter effect. There are some reports in the literature where molecular fluorophores such as Citrazinic acid is formed as a byproduct during synthesis from similar precursor and has been assigned as a possible source of blue emission, whereas citrazinic amide and/or pyridone derivatives emit yellow-green fluorescence [14]. However, in the present case where multistep purification methods are adopted, the presence of such impurities is completely ruled out. Secondly, an aqueous medium is found to inhibit the formation of such yellow-green emitting citrazinic amide and/or pyridine derivatives. This necessitates the need for detailed structural and morphological characterization of YCD that is formed as a major product during hydrothermal synthesis from Ammonium citrate dibasic and Urea

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, morphology and functional group characterization
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the morphology, surface functional groups, and source of yellow emission, a host of analytical techniques were used to characterize the YCD. The TEM and AFM techniques were used for the morphological characterization of CDs. A TEM image of YCD is presented in Fig. 2A which shows the formation of agglomerate with a comprehensive size greater than a few hundred of nanometers. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns unveiled the nearly crystalline nature of the CDs (Fig. 2A, inset). The formation of such aggregated structure is observed by others also during TEM characterization of CDs [23,34]. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2B) revealed the existence of ~ 5–6 nm quasi-spherical CDs with a lattice spacing of ~ 0.33 nm indicating the presence of (002) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [35] closely placed in the form of aggregates. Similar clusters of CD were observed during AFM studies and correlates well with the TEM analysis (Fig. 2C). AFM studies also revealed multiple layers of aggregated CDs stacked on each other. X-ray diffractogram of the YCD showed two diffraction peaks around 28.7° and 39.2° corresponding to (002,100) planes of the graphitic framework (Fig. 2D) [35,36]. The interlayer spacing (d002 and d100) were estimated to be 0.31 and 0.23 nm respectively according to Bragg's law.

The surface functional groups and chemical structure of YCD were analyzed by FTIR and XPS measurements. The FTIR spectrum of YCD (Fig. 3A) showed the presence of a characteristic absorption band in the region of 3349 cm−1 corresponding to asymmetrical and symmetrical NH stretching, whereas the NH in-plane bending and CO stretching vibration at 1588 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 respectively confirmed the presence of amide functionality on the surface of YCD. Such vibrational fingerprints of the amide group have been reported previously during FTIR analysis of CD samples [37–39]. The peak at 1408 cm−1 and 1041 cm−1 could be assigned to C-O-C and CN stretching vibrations, respectively. The XPS spectral analysis also confirmed the presence of the amide groups on CD. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 3B) showed three typical peaks at 285.3 eV (C1S), 531.2 eV (O1S), and 400 eV (N1S) that confirmed the presence of Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), and Nitrogen (N) with an atomic percentage of 59.43, 32.5 and 8.07 respectively. The high resolution deconvoluted C1S spectral peaks correspond to CC/CC (284.7 eV), C-N/C-O (286.8 eV), and amide carbonyl CO (288.6 eV). The O1S deconvoluted spectrum contains peaks for CO (531.1 eV) and CO (533.1 eV) respectively. Further, in the N1S spectrum, 397.7 eV and 400 eV peaks characteristic to the binding energy of pyridine and amide-like nitrogen are present, whereas the 402.3 eV peak is assigned to graphitic nitrogen (Fig. 3C-E). The C1S spectra also have a small peak at 283.1 eV which might be a shake-up signal due to π-π* (HOMO-LUMO) transition of unsaturated carbon [40]. The energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) analysis obtained from TEM of the YCD also confirmed the presence of Carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), and Oxygen (O) atoms, and correlates well with XPS data (Fig. S5, SI).

Based on the above findings it is inferred that YCD is an aggregated structure consisting 3D network of a large number of CDs having a 5–6 nm radius and holding a nearly graphitic structure. The presence of the amide groups on the surface of these individual CDs is also ascertained. The aggregated species dissolve easily in aqueous medium at neutral pH, which on photoexcitation shows AIE in the yellow region with a significant Stoke's shift (~130 nm). The PL excitation spectra have a sharp peak at 405 nm matching with the long-wavelength band of absorption spectra and the PL decay is mono-exponential, hence single species is present in the excited state. The BCD was also characterized using XRD and FTIR techniques. Although the XRD pattern of BCD is identical with YCD, the FTIR spectra are significantly different (Fig. S6, SI). It implies that both YCD and BCD contain similar morphological and structural properties but different functional groups on their surface. The optical spectroscopic results of BCD are typical to that commonly reported, having long-wavelength absorption around 300–350 nm associated with n-π* transition of surface functional groups resulting in emission in the blue region. This makes us believe that the BCD is a non-aggregated free CD that was defected during the chromatographic separation process.

Subsequently, the pH effect on absorption and emission properties of YCD was investigated. The pH of the YCD solution was varied towards basic and acidic pH regions using NaOH and HCl solutions respectively. As the pH of the YCD solution (0.225 mg/ml) was increased, the optical density at 405 nm started decreasing concurrent with the emergence of a new absorption band at 325 nm. In addition, peaks at ~ 250 nm and 270 nm disappeared, giving rise to a new band at 257 nm. The protolytic reaction also resulted in quenching of YCD fluorescence whereas, a blue emission similar to BCD, centered at ~ 430 nm is observed from the new species. Fig. 4A and B shows the pH-dependent variation in the absorption and emission spectra of YCD in the alkaline medium. The pKa value estimated as 10.8 by following the pH-dependent variation of absorption intensity at 405 nm and 325 nm is depicted in Fig. 4C, whereas the pKa from fluorescence intensity changes at 535 nm is estimated to be 10.6 (Fig. 4D). Similar results were obtained when pKa was estimated by monitoring fluorescence intensity at 430 nm of the new species formed in an alkaline medium. Digital images were captured when exposed to a UV-lamp during base titration, which shows a gradual change of PL from yellow to blue color, upon continuous addition of NaOH (Fig. 4E). While varying the pH of YCD solution (7.2 → 12.5) gradual changes in their absorption and emission spectra were observed, however, it was insensitive to pH changes when we moved in the reverse direction (Fig. S7, SI). Such a non-reversible pH effect indicates that the system is undergoing mechanistic structural changes.

Most of the results available in the literature do not report any significant pH-induced changes on the absorption spectrum of CD. To the best of our knowledge, only small spectral shift [30,41] and/or intensity variations [32] were observed in some cases. However, Li et al. has reported the emergence of a new absorption band on changing pH of CD solution, similar to that detected in the present case [42]. They used Neutral Red dye and thiourea to fabricate red-emitting CD. In the past, we have carried out detailed spectroscopic investigations on both neutral and cationic forms of neutral red [43,44]. Interestingly the optical absorption, emission, and pKa of CD reported by Li et al. match with that of the precursor neutral red. Secondly, a 1 kDa dialysis membrane is used by them for purification which may be inadequate [15] for purifying these materials, particularly when the size of their CD is ~ 2.3 nm and that of the precursor, neutral red ~ 1.5 nm [45]. This makes us believe that their observed spectroscopic results are associated with the dye molecules either adsorbed on the surface of CD, trapped within carbonized layers, or simply left in the solution during inadequate purification.

The alkaline CD solutions were further analyzed by TEM, AFM, and DLS as well as through Zeta potential measurements to decipher the mechanism of pH-induced non-reversible changes in the absorption and emission properties. The TEM and AFM measurements indicated disintegration of the aggregated CDs in alkaline medium comparable to the as-synthesized BCD (Fig. S8, SI). The DLS measurements (Fig. S9, SI) also gave direct evidence of YCD disintegration in alkaline medium and supported the findings of microscopic measurements. The optical properties of CD in the alkaline medium are similar to that found typical of free CD in solution. This confirms that the yellow emission from YCD is aggregation-induced. The Zeta potential values (Fig. S10, SI) measured as −2.8 eV (pH 7) and 11 eV (pH 12) under neutral and alkaline conditions do not vary significantly and they are very close to the isoelectric point, therefore the driving force of aggregation is not purely electrostatic in nature.

In acid conditions, the optical density of YCD (0.1 mg/ml) at 405 nm remains unchanged but the emission intensity at 535 nm decreases gradually on reducing the pH (Fig. 5A). The fluorescence lifetime is also quenched from 4.05 ns (pH 7) to 2.91 ns (pH 1.4) (Fig. S11, SI). Following the fluorescence intensity changes at 535 nm, the pKa was estimated to be 2.18 (Fig. 5B). These changes in the acidic medium were reversible and the solution retained its bright yellow color once the pH of the solution is readjusted to neutral. The color transitions were also recorded as digital images on UV illumination of YCD solution during pH-variation from 7.4 to 1.4 (Fig. 5C). The quenching of YCD fluorescence in acidic medium is attributed to hydrogen bonding observed by others also for CDs with similar surface functional groups [37–39]. Hydrogen bonding opens a new pathway for nonradiative decay of species in the excited state, which results in quenching of fluorescence intensity and the lifetime.

To understand the mechanism of pH-induced aggregation/disintegration and quenching, XRD, FTIR and NMR spectroscopic studies were performed. The overall XRD pattern (Fig. S12, SI), hence crystal structure of the CD was found to be identical at different pH conditions. However, the FTIR spectrum of our CD at different pH conditions that is presented in Fig. 6A exhibit significant variations. Under acidic conditions the NH stretching, in-plane bending and CO stretching vibration bands became sharp, more pronounced and frequency shifted. This indicates the involvement of the amide group in the hydrogen bond formation under acidic conditions [46]. The FTIR spectra in alkaline medium show strong OH stretching and bending bands at 3433 cm−1 and 1306 cm−1 respectively. The carboxylic CO stretching and NH bending at 1655 cm−1 and amine III CN stretching band at 1460 cm−1 which is nearly identical to that of BCD (Fig. S6B, SI), except pH-induced frequency shifts. Interestingly, Yang et al [47]. also made nearly identical observations for the CD with amine, hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups synthesized from similar precursor ammonium citrate. The FTIR study of CDs synthesized from ammonium citrate dibasic by Sun et al [48]. again revealed the presence of similar functional groups and the formation of new products with prolongation of reaction time. Also, 1H NMR of aggregated YCD displayed a broad peak at 4.30 ppm corresponding to amide Hydrogen, whereas for disintegrated BCD, this peak is absent with the emergence of a new peak at 3.64 ppm corresponding to amine functionalities (Fig. S13 - S16, SI)·13C NMR of both aggregated YCD and disintegrated BCD showed the characteristic peak of carboxylic acid/amide carbon at ~ 175 ppm. From the above observation, it is evident that the YCD at neutral pH has amide functionality that disintegrate into amine and carboxylic acid at alkaline pH. The optical properties of both the CDs synthesized from either ammonium citrate or ammonium citrate dibasic match with BCD as well as our alkaline CD solution. The fluorescence of free CD in alkaline medium (pH ~ 12) follows bi-exponential decays giving time constants of 1.36 and 6.08 ns respectively. Apart from small variations associated with pH changes, these results along with FTIR data matches well with that of BCD.

Based on above observations, we propose a plausible mechanism of synthesis and pH-induced changes in the alkaline medium of YCD. Once a mixture of Ammonium citrate dibasic and Urea in the molar ratio of 1:3 was placed in an autoclave reactor at high temperature (180 °C), in the initial phase CDs with an average size of 5–6 nm are formed which contains a large number of amino and carboxylic groups on their surface. Due to prolonged heating up to 10 h, intra-particle condensation reaction between amine and carboxylic surface group forms amide conjugated networks of CDs producing interconnected clusters of up to micron size. The energy stabilized aggregated state, which could be represented as shown in Fig. 6B; absorbs at 405 nm and decays single exponentially at 535 nm similar to that observed by others in similar cases [25,39]. These 3D networks of CDs are formed and stable at neutral pH. However, in strongly alkaline conditions upon exposure to NaOH, the amide bonds undergo hydrolysis to disintegrate the aggregates by producing free CDs with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on their surface [49]. Since amides are more polar as compared to acids and amines, the solubility of YCD is better than BCD in the aqueous medium. The presence of urea in a very high proportion during the current synthesis seems to be responsible for variation in optical characteristics of pristine YCD as compared to others [47,48] using a similar precursor. The proposed mechanism of synthesis and pH-induced changes in the alkaline medium of YCD is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Further, to explore the potential applications of YCD, we prepared a thin film with a physical mixture of Polyvinyl alcohol and YCD which shows brilliant yellow luminescent upon 365 nm UV lamp illumination that may find use in packaging, phosphor layer and other applications. We also demonstrate that YCD could be used for latent fingerprint imaging and as a pH sensitive ink that display color conversion under UV-light from yellow to blue upon spraying with NaOH solution (Fig. S17, SI).

Given the wide range of organic compounds available to us, the scope of synthesis of CD is virtually infinite. The applicability of such CDs is a function of the substrates chosen that dictate the surface states and hence their photophysical aspects. However, the post-synthetic purification of CD is of paramount importance that essentially eliminates molecular fluorophores responsible for several artifacts including PL outcome. As a proof of concept, we carried out an extensive purification strategy for our newly synthesized CD to evidence proton-induced interesting morphisms of the CD leading to extensive changes in emissivity. Highly luminescent CDs synthesized from Ammonium citrate dibasic and Urea using the hydrothermal technique show remarkable absorption and emission behavior on varying the pH. In the course of synthesis initially individual CDs with amino and carboxylic functional groups are generated, which conjugate by condensation through amide bonding to form micron size 3D cluster due to prolonged heating at high temperature. This is ascertained from the FTIR and NMR spectra of free and the aggregated CDs. During multi-step purification of CD to separate free and aggregated dots gel electrophoresis is an excellent choice due to the difference in polarity of their surface groups. The pH-induced changes in optical properties of YCD are quite interesting. While pristine YCD exhibits AIE in the yellow region at neutral pH, its emission quenches on moving towards acidic medium due to hydrogen bonding associated with the amide group. In alkaline medium, however, the 3D cluster of YCD breaks down due to hydrolysis of amide bonds, producing free CDs with amino, carboxylic, and hydroxyl groups on their surface, which exhibit different optical properties than the aggregates. Synthesis of such aggregated CDs with high AIE has great potential for applications as phosphors in electroluminescent devices, sensors and forensic material. The green hydrothermal synthesis enhances their dual applicability as biological markers where pH-induced changes in optical behavior can also be exploited.
Suman Nayak – Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft.
Prolay Das – Validation, Supervision, Writing - review and editing.

Manoj K. Singh- Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing - review and editing. The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to IIT Patna and BARC, Mumbai for infrastructure and experimental facilities. SN is thankful to IIT Patna for fellowship. Special thanks to Dr. P. A. Hassan, BARC for his help in DLS measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data and experimental details to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colcom.2021.100537.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ding, H.; Yu, S.B.; Wei, J.S.; Xiong, H.M.; Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism. ACS Nano, 2016, 10, 484-491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence in carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full color carbon dots through surface engineering for constructing white light-emitting diodes
